title: The People's Republic of China and IR: Foreign Policy of a Rising Power
further_info: Recommended Reading Breslin, Shaun/Xiao, Ren (Hg.) (2022). China debates its global role. Chinese scholars on Chinese scholarship. London/New York, Routledge. Garver, John W. (2016). China's Quest. The History of the Foreign Relations of the People's Republic. New York, Oxford University Press. Goddard, Stacie E. (2018). Embedded Revisionism: Networks, Institutions, and Challenges to World Order. International Organization 72 (4), 763–797. Heilmann, Sebastian (Hg.) (2016). Das politische System der Volksrepublik China. 3. Aufl. Wiesbaden, Springer VS. Huang, Haifeng (2025). Reckoning with Reality: Correcting National Overconfidence in a Rising Power. International Organization 79 (4), 672–711. https://doi.org/10.1017/S0020818325100969. Jones, Lee/Hameiri, Shahar (2021). Fractured China. How state transformation is shaping China's rise. Cambridge, United Kingdom/New York, NY, Cambridge University Press. Jost, Tyler (2024). Bureaucracies at war. The institutional origins of miscalculation. Cambridge, United Kingdom/New York, NY, Cambridge University Press. Koo, John N. L./Quek, Kai (2024). Win-Win Deescalation. International Studies Quarterly 68 (4). https://doi.org/10.1093/isq/sqae148. Sørensen, Georg/Møller, Jørgen/Jackson, Robert H. (2022). Introduction to international relations. Theories and approaches. Oxford, United Kingdom, Oxford University Press. Thies, Cameron G./Nieman, Mark David (2018). Rising powers and foreign policy revisionism. Understanding BRICS identity and behavior through time. Ann Arbor, Mich., University of Michigan Press. Weiss, Jessica Chen (2014). Powerful patriots. Nationalist protest in China's foreign relations. New York, Oxford University Press. Ye, Min (2020). The Belt Road and Beyond. Cambridge University Press. Zhao, Suisheng (2023). The dragon roars back. Transformational leaders and dynamics of Chinese foreign policy. Stanford, California, Stanford University Press.
